subsections.title: Four Pillars of OOP
subsections.keyRule: Encapsulation = hide data behind methods. Inheritance = reuse code from parent class. Polymorphism = one name, many behaviors. Abstraction = show what, hide how. All four work together in every well-designed Java class.

terms.what: Encapsulation means wrapping data (fields) and behavior (methods) together inside a class, and controlling access using access modifiers (private/protected/public).
terms.why: Without encapsulation, any code can directly change any field — you lose control of your object's state. Encapsulation protects your object from invalid states.
terms.how: Make fields private. Provide public getters/setters with validation. Example: private int age; public void setAge(int age) { if(age < 0) throw new IllegalArgumentException(); this.age = age; }
terms.where: Every well-designed Java class. Spring beans, JPA entities, DTOs — all should encapsulate their state.
terms.when: Always. The only exception is records (Java 14+) where fields are final and public by design.
terms.issue: Public fields allow any code to set invalid values (age = -5). Breaking encapsulation makes code hard to test, debug, and refactor.
terms.fix: Private fields + public getters/setters with validation. Or use Builder pattern for complex construction. Lombok @Getter @Setter auto-generate.

star.s: Bug where user age becomes negative.
star.a: Field was public — any code could set it. Made private. Added validation in setter.
star.r: Invalid ages impossible.

terms.what: Inheritance lets a child class get all fields and methods of a parent class using the 'extends' keyword. The child reuses and extends the parent's behavior.
terms.why: Avoids code duplication. Common behavior goes in the parent class once — all child classes get it automatically.
terms.how: class Animal { void eat() {...} } class Dog extends Animal { void bark() {...} } — Dog has both eat() and bark(). Dog IS-A Animal.
terms.where: Class hierarchies: Vehicle→Car→ElectricCar, Exception→RuntimeException→YourException, JpaRepository chain in Spring Data.
terms.when: Use when a true IS-A relationship exists. Do NOT use inheritance just for code reuse — use composition instead. Prefer composition over inheritance.
terms.issue: Deep inheritance chains (5+ levels) make code hard to follow. Changes in parent break all children — fragile base class problem.
terms.fix: Keep inheritance shallow (max 2-3 levels). Use interfaces + composition for flexibility. 'Prefer composition over inheritance' is a core Java best practice.

star.s: Design review — inheritance chain 6 levels deep.
star.a: Flattened to 2 levels. Extracted shared behavior into helper classes (composition).
star.r: Code much easier to understand and test.

terms.what: Polymorphism means 'many forms'. One method name behaves differently depending on the actual object type. Two types: compile-time (overloading) and runtime (overriding).
terms.why: Write code that works with multiple types without knowing the exact type at compile time. The right method is called automatically based on the actual object.
terms.how: Runtime: Animal a = new Dog(); a.makeSound(); — calls Dog's makeSound() not Animal's. Compile-time: print(int), print(String) — compiler picks the right one.
terms.where: Every interface and abstract class usage. Spring's @Repository, @Service, @Controller are polymorphic — you code to the interface.
terms.when: Use runtime polymorphism (overriding + interfaces) for extensible, testable designs. Use overloading sparingly — too many overloads can be confusing.

terms.what: Abstraction means showing only what an object does, hiding how it does it. Users see the interface (what), not the implementation (how).
terms.why: Reduces complexity. You use a List without knowing if it is an ArrayList or LinkedList. You call service.findUser() without caring about the SQL behind it.
terms.how: Achieved through abstract classes and interfaces. define the WHAT (method signatures). Concrete classes implement the HOW.
terms.where: Every service interface in Spring. Repository layer hides database details. API contracts hide internal implementation.
terms.when: Abstract whenever the implementation might change or when multiple implementations exist. Inject interfaces, not concrete classes.
terms.issue: Over-abstraction adds unnecessary layers. If a class has only one implementation and never changes, the interface is overhead.
terms.fix: Add abstraction when there are multiple implementations OR when you need to mock for testing. Don't abstract everything just because you can.

star.s: Service class directly uses specific database driver code.
star.a: Extracted interface. Service uses interface. Tests inject mock.
star.r: Fully testable. DB can be swapped.

subsections.title: Abstract Class vs Interface
subsections.keyRule: 'extends' = IS-A. 'has-a' = composition (preferred). super() calls parent constructor. super.method() calls parent method. final class = cannot extend. final method = cannot override. @Override catches typos at compile time.

terms.what: An abstract class is a partially implemented class. It can have abstract methods (no body) AND concrete methods (with body). Cannot be instantiated directly.
terms.why: Provides a template with some code already written. Child classes inherit the concrete parts and must implement the abstract parts.
terms.how: abstract class Shape { abstract double area(); void printArea() { System.out.println(area()); } } — printArea() is inherited by all shapes for free.
terms.where: Class hierarchies where subclasses share code AND state. Template Method pattern. Base service or controller classes in Spring.
terms.when: Use abstract class when: subclasses share concrete code or fields, you want to force subclasses to implement certain methods, and you need constructors.
terms.issue: Abstract class locks you into single inheritance. A class can extend only ONE abstract class — limits flexibility.
terms.fix: If you only need a contract (no shared code), use interface instead. If you need shared code AND contract, abstract class is right.

star.s: 5 payment processors all duplicate validation logic.
star.a: Created abstract PaymentProcessor with validate() concrete and process() abstract.
star.r: 50% code reduction. Validation logic in one place.

terms.what: An interface defines a pure contract — a list of methods that implementing classes MUST provide. Before Java 8: no method bodies. Java 8+: default and static methods allowed.
terms.why: Interfaces enable multiple 'contracts' for one class. A class can implement 5 interfaces but extend only 1 class. Enables loose coupling and testability.
terms.how: interface Flyable { void fly(); } interface Swimmable { void swim(); } class Duck implements Flyable, Swimmable { ... } — Duck can do both.
terms.where: Spring @Repository, @Service interfaces. Java Collections (List, Map, Set). Functional interfaces for lambdas. Any API contract.
terms.when: Use interface when: defining a contract, enabling multiple implementations, enabling mocking in tests, or adding capabilities to unrelated classes.
terms.issue: Before Java 8, adding a new method to an interface broke all implementing classes. Still an issue without default methods.
terms.fix: Add new methods as 'default' methods in the interface — existing implementations get a default behavior and don't break.

star.s: Adding audit() method to Auditable interface breaks 20 implementing classes.
star.a: Made it a default method with basic impl. Classes that need custom audit override it.
star.r: Zero compilation breaks.

terms.what: Default methods let interfaces have method bodies. Any class implementing the interface gets the default behavior automatically without adding code.
terms.why: Before Java 8, adding a method to an interface broke every implementing class. Default methods allow interface evolution without breaking changes.
terms.how: interface Logger { void log(String msg); default void logError(String msg) { log('ERROR: ' + msg); } } — implementing classes get logError() for free.
terms.where: Java 8 Collections API used default methods heavily to add forEach(), stream(), etc. to List/Map/Set without breaking existing code.
terms.when: Use default methods to add new capabilities to an existing interface without forcing all implementors to change. Or for optional behavior with a sensible default.
terms.issue: If a class implements two interfaces that both have a default method with the same name, compilation error — ambiguity.
terms.fix: Override the conflicting default method in the implementing class to resolve which one to use.

star.s: Need to add retry() to PaymentGateway interface. 15 implementations exist.
star.a: Made retry() a default method with 3-retry logic. Implementations override only if they need custom retry.
star.r: No compilation breaks.

terms.what: Key differences: Abstract class can have instance fields, constructors, and concrete methods. Interface cannot have instance fields or constructors. Class extends ONE abstract class but implements MULTIPLE interfaces.
terms.why: Understanding the difference helps you pick the right tool for the right job — more flexibility vs more code sharing.
terms.how: Rule of thumb: Interface = CAN-DO contract (Flyable, Serializable, Runnable). Abstract class = IS-A relationship with shared code (Vehicle base class).
terms.where: Most modern Java design: interfaces for contracts, abstract classes for template patterns. Spring repositories use interfaces.
terms.when: Choose abstract class when subclasses genuinely share state and code. Choose interface when you need multiple type contracts or loose coupling.
terms.issue: New developers overuse abstract classes because they seem powerful — but they create tight coupling and limit flexibility.
terms.fix: Default to interfaces. Add an abstract class only when you have real code to share. Prefer composition over inheritance anyway.

star.s: Interview question: when to use abstract class vs interface?
star.a: Interface = contract for unrelated classes (Serializable). Abstract class = shared code for related classes (Animal). Java 8 closes the gap with default methods.
star.r: Correct and complete answer.

terms.what: final on a class means no subclassing. final on a method means no overriding. final on a variable means assign once — never changed.
terms.why: Protects critical behavior. String is final — no one can make a fake String class. final method prevents subclasses from breaking a parent's guaranteed logic.
terms.how: final class String. final void criticalMethod(). final int MAX = 100; — all assignments after first = compile error.
terms.where: Immutable value objects, security-sensitive classes, constants (public static final), performance (JIT inlines final methods).
terms.when: Make a class final when you never want it extended and want to guarantee its behavior. Make local variables final when their value should not change after assignment.
terms.issue: Declaring everything final 'for safety' without reason makes code hard to test — you cannot mock a final class with standard Mockito.
terms.fix: Mockito 3.4+ can mock final classes with mockito-inline. Or extract an interface. Make final only what truly must not change.

star.s: Security class was subclassed and overridden to bypass auth check.
star.a: Made SecurityValidator final. No subclassing possible.
star.r: Bypass impossible by design.

terms.what: @Override tells the compiler: this method must override a parent method. If the method name or signature is wrong, compile error — not a silent new method.
terms.why: Without @Override, a typo like 'equals(Object0 o)' silently creates a new method instead of overriding Object.equals() — very hard to find bug.
terms.how: @Override public boolean equals(Object o) { ... } — compiler verifies the signature matches a parent. Remove @Override = no compile check.
terms.where: Every method intended to override a parent method or implement an interface. Always use — zero cost, huge safety benefit.
terms.when: Always. No exception. Even if you are 100% sure the signature is right — @Override is cheap insurance.
terms.issue: Forgetting @Override on toString() — misspelled as Tostring() — silently creates a useless method. Default Object.toString() still called.
terms.fix: Enable 'Missing @Override' warnings in your IDE and SonarQube. Add @Override to all intended overrides.

star.s: Object not printing correctly — custom toString() never called.
star.a: Method was 'Tostring()' — wrong case. No @Override so no compile error. Added @Override. Fixed typo.
star.r: toString() now called correctly.

terms.what: In Java, instance methods are overridden (runtime polymorphism). Static methods are hidden — the version called depends on the declared type, not the actual object type.
terms.why: Understanding this prevents confusion when dealing with static methods in class hierarchies — common interview trap.
terms.how: class Parent { static void show() { print('Parent'); } } class Child extends Parent { static void show() { print('Child'); } } Parent p = new Child(); p.show() → prints 'Parent' (hiding, not overriding).
terms.where: Any static method that appears in both parent and child class. Static methods belong to the class, not instances.
terms.when: Avoid calling static methods through instance references — call through class name (Parent.show(), Child.show()). This makes hiding behavior obvious.
terms.issue: Calling Parent p = new Child(); p.show() expects 'Child' but gets 'Parent'. Method hiding does not use virtual dispatch.
terms.fix: Always call static methods via ClassName.method() — never via an instance. This prevents confusion from method hiding.

terms.what: IS-A is inheritance: Dog IS-A Animal. HAS-A is composition: Car HAS-A Engine. IS- A = extends/implements. HAS-A = field holding another object.
terms.why: Choosing wrong leads to bad design. 'Prefer composition over inheritance' is a core Java principle.
terms.how: IS-A: class Dog extends Animal. HAS-A: class Car { Engine engine; } — Car is not a kind of Engine, it has one.
terms.where: Every class design decision. Spring beans typically HAS-A dependency (injected via constructor).
terms.when: Use IS-A only for true type relationships. Use HAS-A when you want to reuse behavior without claiming to be a type of that thing.
terms.issue: IS-A misuse: Stack extends Vector in Java — Stack IS-NOT a Vector, it just reuses its methods. This is why java.util.Stack has wrong behavior (get(0), etc.).
terms.fix: Use HAS-A for code reuse. IS-A only when truly a subtype. The Stack mistake is why Deque is now recommended.

star.s: Design review: Employee extends Address.
star.a: Employee IS- NOT an Address. Employee HAS-A Address. Changed to composition field.
star.r: Correct model, flexible design.

terms.what: Java allows an overriding method to return a more specific (subtype) return type than the parent method. This is covariant return type.
terms.why: More specific return types avoid unnecessary casts. Java 5+ supports this.
terms.how: class Animal { Animal create() { return new Animal(); } } class Dog extends Animal { @Override Dog create() { return new Dog(); } } — Dog is more specific than Animal.
terms.where: Factory methods in class hierarchies, builder patterns, cloning methods.
terms.when: Use covariant return when the override naturally returns a more specific type and callers benefit from not casting.
terms.issue: Forgetting @Override with covariant return type — compiler may not catch it as an override if you forget @Override.
terms.fix: Always use @Override. Covariant return is valid and compiler checks it correctly when @Override is present.

star.s: Callers casting Animal to Dog after calling create().
star.a: Overrode create() in Dog to return Dog. Callers now get Dog without cast.
star.r: Cleaner code, no runtime ClassCastException risk.

terms.what: At runtime, Java calls the overridden method of the actual object's class, not the declared type. This is dynamic dispatch — the method is resolved at runtime.
terms.why: Enables polymorphism — you write code against an interface/parent and it works correctly for all subclasses.
terms.how: Animal a = new Dog(); a.speak() — compiler sees Animal.speak(). JVM at runtime sees the actual object is Dog. Calls Dog.speak(). This is the entire foundation of OOP.
terms.where: Every interface usage, every abstract method call, every Spring bean method call.
terms.when: Works automatically whenever a method is overridden. Static methods, private methods, and final methods do NOT participate in dynamic dispatch.
terms.issue: Calling a non-final method from a constructor executes the child's overridden version — child may not be fully initialized yet. Can cause NPE or wrong behavior.
terms.fix: Do not call overridable methods from constructors. Use a factory method or post- construction init() instead.

star.s: Interview: Animal a = new Dog(); a.eat() — which eat() is called?
star.a: Dog.eat() — runtime type is Dog. Dynamic dispatch uses actual object type, not declared type.
star.r: Demonstrates understanding of OOP.

subsections.title: SOLID Principles
subsections.keyRule: S = Single Responsibility. O = Open/Closed. L = Liskov Substitution. I = Interface Segregation. D = Dependency Inversion. These five principles make code maintainable, testable, and extensible. Violating any one leads to fragile, hard-to-change code.

terms.what: A class should have only ONE reason to change — one job. Not 'do everything'. Not 'utility class for all things'.
terms.why: When a class has one job, changing that job affects only that class. Multiple jobs = multiple reasons to change = more risk of breaking unrelated things.
terms.how: OrderService should process orders. It should NOT also send emails, log to files, AND validate payment. Break those into EmailService, OrderLogger, PaymentValidator.
terms.where: Every class in your codebase. Spring's @Service, @Repository, @Controller separation IS SRP applied at the layer level.
terms.when: If you describe a class with the word 'AND', it likely violates SRP. Split it.
terms.issue: God classes — one class doing 20 things — are the most common SRP violation. Hard to test, hard to change, impossible to understand.
terms.fix: Split by responsibility. Each class has one clear purpose. If a class is over 300 lines, it probably violates SRP.

star.s: UserManager class handles login, registration, email, and reporting.
star.a: Split into UserAuthService, UserRegistrationService, EmailService, UserReportService.
star.r: Each class easy to test and change independently.

terms.what: Classes should be open for extension (add new behavior) but closed for modification (don't change existing code). Add, don't edit.
terms.why: Changing existing code risks breaking existing tests and behavior. Adding new code is safer — existing tests still pass.
terms.how: Use polymorphism. Instead of if-else for each payment type, have a PaymentProcessor interface. Add a new payment type by adding a new class — no existing code changes.
terms.where: Strategy pattern, factory pattern, Spring beans with interfaces. Anywhere you might need to extend behavior in the future.
terms.when: Apply OCP when you anticipate extensions. Don't over-engineer if the behavior truly never changes.
terms.issue: if-else or switch-case blocks that grow with every new feature violate OCP — every new type requires editing existing code.
terms.fix: Replace if-else with polymorphism. Each 'type' is a class. Add new types by adding new classes, not editing existing switch.

star.s: addDiscount() has 10 if-else branches for each customer type.
star.a: Created DiscountStrategy interface. Each customer type is a class. New type = new class.
star.r: Zero changes to existing code for new types.

terms.what: If S is a subtype of T, you must be able to use S wherever T is used without breaking the program. Subclass must honor the parent's contract.
terms.why: If subclasses break parent behavior, polymorphism becomes unreliable. Code that works with the parent type breaks when given a subclass.
terms.how: Classic violation: class Square extends Rectangle. Rectangle.setWidth() changes width only. But Square must keep width=height, so it overrides setWidth() to also set height — breaks Rectangle's contract.
terms.where: Every inheritance and interface implementation. Especially important in generic/abstract APIs.
terms.when: Any time you override a parent method, ask: does this still honor the parent's contract? Pre/post conditions must not weaken.
terms.issue: Overriding a method to throw UnsupportedOperationException violates LSP. Caller expects the method to work — not throw.
terms.fix: If the subclass cannot honor the parent's contract, inheritance is wrong. Use composition instead. Design contracts carefully before creating hierarchies.

terms.what: Do not force a class to implement methods it does not need. Split large interfaces into small focused ones.
terms.why: Fat interfaces force implementing classes to provide empty or fake implementations for methods they don't use — wastes code and confuses readers.
terms.how: Instead of one Animal interface with fly(), swim(), run() — split into Flyable, Swimmable, Runnable. Dog implements Runnable+Swimmable but not Flyable.
terms.where: Any interface with more than 4-5 methods. Check if all implementing classes really use all methods.
terms.when: Apply when you notice implementing classes have empty or throws-exception method bodies — sign they were forced to implement something they don't need.
terms.issue: Large interface like Worker with work(), eat(), sleep() — a Robot class forced to implement eat() and sleep() which make no sense.
terms.fix: Split Worker into Workable with work(). Robot only implements Workable. Humans implement Workable + Eatable + Sleepable.

star.s: Printer class implements AllInOne interface but throws for scan() and fax() — printer-only device.
star.a: Split into Printable, Scannable, Faxable. Printer implements only Printable.
star.r: Clean, no fake implementations.

terms.what: High-level modules should not depend on low-level modules. Both should depend on abstractions (interfaces). Don't depend on concrete classes — depend on interfaces.
terms.why: If high-level code directly uses a low-level class, changing the low-level class breaks the high-level class. Interface in between protects the high-level code.
terms.how: BAD: OrderService creates new MySQLOrderRepository(). GOOD: OrderService takes OrderRepository (interface) in constructor. Spring injects the real or mock implementation.
terms.where: Spring Dependency Injection is DIP in practice. @Autowired fields should always be interface types. Test with mock implementations.
terms.when: Always inject dependencies through constructors or interfaces. Never 'new' a concrete dependency inside a service class.
terms.issue: new ConcreteClass() inside a service = tight coupling. Cannot unit test without starting the real DB. Cannot swap implementations.
terms.fix: Inject via constructor: OrderService(OrderRepository repo). Annotate with @Autowired in Spring. Tests inject mocks. Production injects real DB implementation.

star.s: Service class untestable — does new OrderRepository() inside constructor.
star.a: Moved repository to constructor parameter. Spring @Autowired injects real. Tests inject Mockito mock.
star.r: Full unit test coverage without DB.

subsections.title: Design Patterns
subsections.keyRule: Creational: how objects are created (Singleton, Factory, Builder). Structural: how objects are composed (Decorator, Proxy, Adapter). Behavioral: how objects communicate (Strategy, Observer, Command, Template Method). Know one real Java/Spring example for each in interviews.

terms.what: Singleton ensures only ONE instance of a class exists in the entire JVM. All code that needs it gets the same instance.
terms.why: Some resources must be shared — database connection pool, configuration, logging system. Creating multiple instances wastes resources or causes bugs.

terms.what: Factory creates objects for you based on a type or condition — you ask for the object, Factory decides which concrete class to instantiate.
terms.why: Decouples object creation from usage. The caller does not need to know which concrete class to use — just asks the factory.
terms.how: NotificationFactory.create('EMAIL') → EmailNotification. NotificationFactory.create('SMS') → SmsNotification. Caller just uses Notification interface.
terms.where: Spring's ApplicationContext is a factory. JDBC DriverManager.getConnection() is a factory. Document parsers based on file type.
terms.when: Use when: the exact class to create depends on runtime data, or when creation logic is complex and should be centralized.
terms.issue: Factory with large if-else/switch violates OCP — adding a new type requires editing the factory.
terms.fix: Use registry pattern: Map<String, Supplier<Notification>> registry. Register each type. factory.create(type) does map.get(type).get(). Adding new type = adding to registry.

star.s: Adding new report format requires editing ReportFactory switch.
star.a: Changed to Map<String, Supplier<Report>> registry. New format = new entry in registry.
star.r: OCP followed. Zero changes to factory code.

terms.what: Builder lets you create complex objects step by step. Instead of a constructor with 10 parameters, you call methods for each property you want to set.
terms.why: Constructors with many parameters are hard to read and error-prone (wrong order). Builder makes construction readable and safe.
terms.how: User user = User.builder().name('Alice').age(30).email('a@b.com').build(); — clear, readable. Missing optional fields get defaults. Builder validates on build().
terms.where: Lombok @Builder generates it. HttpRequest.newBuilder() in Java 11. UriComponentsBuilder in Spring. Any class with 4+ constructor parameters.
terms.when: Use when: more than 4 parameters in a constructor, some parameters are optional, need to validate after all params are set.
terms.issue: Forgetting to call build() gives you the Builder, not the object. Mutable Builder state can be problematic if shared.
terms.fix: Make build() validate required fields. Lombok @Builder generates it with less code. Check IDE warnings if you assign Builder result without calling build().

star.s: Order constructor has 8 parameters — easy to pass them in wrong order.
star.a: Added @Builder (Lombok). Order.builder().customerId(c).items(items).total(t).build().
star.r: Readable, order-independent, validated.

terms.what: Strategy lets you swap algorithms at runtime. Define a family of algorithms, put each in a class, make them interchangeable through an interface.
terms.why: Instead of one big class with if-else for each algorithm, each algorithm is its own class. Swap them at runtime without changing the calling code.

terms.what: Observer lets objects subscribe to events. When the subject changes, it notifies all subscribers automatically without knowing who they are.
terms.why: Decouples event producer from consumers. Adding new reactions to an event requires only a new subscriber — no changes to the event producer.
terms.how: Subject has List<Observer> observers. On change, calls observer.update() for each. Observer implements the update() method.
terms.where: Spring ApplicationEvents (@EventListener). GUI event listeners. Domain events in DDD. Kafka consumers are Observers at scale.
terms.when: Use when multiple parts of the system react to the same event and should not depend on each other.
terms.issue: Too many observers can make event flow hard to trace — 'I have no idea what order things happen.' Also, observers can throw and block others.
terms.fix: Use Spring's @EventListener with @Async for non-critical observers. Use Kafka for cross-service event broadcasting. Log event dispatch for tracing.

star.s: After order placed, 5 things need to happen: email, inventory, loyalty points, analytics, audit.
star.a: Publish OrderPlacedEvent. 5 @EventListener methods.
star.r: Each handler independent. Easy to add #6 without touching order logic.

terms.what: Decorator adds behavior to an object dynamically by wrapping it with another object that has the same interface. Stack decorators for multiple additions.
terms.why: Alternative to inheritance for adding behavior. More flexible — combine decorators in any order at runtime.
terms.how: InputStream → BufferedInputStream(FileInputStream) → wraps FileInputStream and adds buffering. Same interface, new behavior added by wrapping.
terms.where: Java I/O streams are all decorators. Spring Security filter chain. HTTP request/response wrappers. Caching layers around repository calls.
terms.when: Use when you need to add behavior to individual objects without affecting others, or when subclassing would create too many combinations.
terms.issue: Stacking many decorators makes debugging hard — many layers of indirection.
terms.fix: Keep decorator chains short. Use logging at key decorator boundaries. Spring AOP is a cleaner approach for cross-cutting concerns like caching and security.

star.s: Need to add caching and logging to an existing service without changing it.
star.a: Created CachingOrderService and LoggingOrderService decorators wrapping OrderService interface.
star.r: Zero changes to original. Behavior added via wrapping.

subsections.title: Structural & Behavioral Patterns
subsections.keyRule: Structural patterns organize how objects/classes are composed. Behavioral patterns define how objects communicate and share responsibility. Know: Proxy (control access), Adapter (interface conversion), Facade (simplify API), Template Method (skeleton algorithm), Command (encapsulate request), Chain of Responsibility (pass along chain).

terms.what: Proxy is a placeholder that controls access to another object. The proxy has the same interface as the real object — callers don't know they are talking to a proxy.
terms.why: Add logic before/after method calls without changing the real object: security checks, caching, lazy initialization, logging.
terms.how: interface Service { void doWork(); } class ServiceProxy implements Service { private Service real; void doWork() { log(); real.doWork(); log(); } }
terms.where: Spring AOP is proxy-based — @Transactional, @Cacheable, @Async all work by wrapping beans in proxy objects. JDBC connection pool proxies. Hibernate lazy loading.
terms.when: Use proxy when: adding cross-cutting concerns without modifying the target, lazy initialization, access control.
terms.issue: Spring proxy only intercepts calls made from outside the bean. Calling @Transactional method from within the same bean bypasses the proxy — no transaction!

terms.what: Adapter converts one interface into another that the client expects. Like a power adapter — same power, different plug shape.
terms.why: When you need to use a class that has an incompatible interface. Instead of changing either side, write an Adapter.
terms.how: interface Target { void request(); } class LegacyService { void oldRequest() {...} } class Adapter implements Target { LegacyService ls = new LegacyService(); void request() { ls.oldRequest(); } }
terms.where: Integrating legacy systems, third-party libraries, bridging between two incompatible APIs.
terms.when: When you cannot modify the existing class (legacy, third-party) but need to fit it into a new interface.
terms.issue: Adapters add an extra layer — too many adapters for the same thing indicates bad original API design.
terms.fix: Document adapters clearly. Use them surgically at integration boundaries only.

star.s: Legacy payment API has completely different method signatures than new system.
star.a: Adapter class wraps legacy service, implements new PaymentGateway interface.
star.r: New system uses legacy API through clean interface.

terms.what: Facade provides a simple, unified interface to a complex subsystem. Hides complexity. Callers use the facade — they don't know how many classes are involved behind it.
terms.why: Reduces dependencies and complexity for callers. Makes subsystems easier to use and understand.
terms.how: class OrderFacade { void placeOrder(Order o) { inventory.reserve(o); payment.charge(o); shipping.schedule(o); email.confirm(o); } } — caller just calls placeOrder().
terms.where: Service layer in Spring that combines multiple repositories and services. SDK entry points. API gateways.
terms.when: Use when: a complex subsystem needs a simple interface for common use cases, or when you want to layer subsystems.
terms.issue: Facade can become a God object if it does too much. If it grows to 50 methods, it is just another complex class.
terms.fix: Keep facade focused. Each facade for one domain. Don't expose every capability — only common use cases.

star.s: Controller calls 5 different services for one operation.
star.a: OrderFacade bundles all 5. Controller calls one method.
star.r: Controller simple, facade testable independently.

terms.what: Template Method defines the skeleton of an algorithm in a base class, letting subclasses fill in specific steps without changing the algorithm's structure.
terms.why: The overall algorithm stays consistent. Only the variable parts change per subclass. No code duplication for the fixed parts.
terms.how: abstract class DataProcessor { final void process() { readData(); processData(); writeData(); } abstract void processData(); } — readData/writeData are inherited, processData is custom.
terms.where: Spring's JdbcTemplate, RestTemplate are Template Method pattern. Any framework 'template' class.
terms.when: Use when multiple classes share the same algorithm structure but differ in specific steps. Put common steps in abstract parent, let children override only what varies.
terms.issue: Template Method ties subclasses to the parent's algorithm structure — hard to change the order of steps without affecting all subclasses.

terms.what: Command encapsulates a request as an object. This allows: queuing, logging, undoing requests, and passing actions as objects.
terms.why: Decouples the sender of a request from the receiver. The sender doesn't need to know HOW the request is executed.
terms.how: interface Command { void execute(); void undo(); } class SaveCommand implements Command { void execute() { save(); } void undo() { rollback(); } } — commands stored in a list for undo.
terms.where: Undo/redo systems, transaction log, macro recording, GUI actions, message queues.
terms.when: Use when: you need undo/redo, command queuing, command logging, or need to parameterize actions.
terms.issue: Too many tiny command classes for simple operations adds overhead.
terms.fix: For simple cases, use lambdas: Runnable command = () -> save(); queue.add(command); — lambda IS a command.

star.s: Text editor needs undo/redo.
star.a: Command interface with execute/undo. Each action is a Command. Stack<Command> undoStack. Undo = pop + call undo().
star.r: Unlimited undo/redo with minimal change to editor.

terms.what: Chain of Responsibility passes a request along a chain of handlers. Each handler decides to handle it or pass it to the next. No one handler must handle everything.
terms.why: Decouples senders from receivers. Allows dynamic chains. Multiple handlers can process a request independently.
terms.how: abstract class Handler { Handler next; void handle(Request r) { if(canHandle(r)) doHandle(r); else if(next != null) next.handle(r); } }
terms.where: Spring Security filter chain (each filter handles auth/authz step by step). HTTP request processing. Logging levels. Support tier escalation.
terms.when: Use when: more than one handler might process a request, the handler is not known at design time, or you want to configure processing dynamically.
terms.issue: Request can fall off the end of the chain if no handler can handle it — silent no- op.
terms.fix: Add a default/fallback handler at the end of the chain. Log unhandled requests.

star.s: Support tickets routed through level 1, 2, 3 support manually.
star.a: Chain of Responsibility — each level checks if it can resolve, passes up if not.
star.r: Automatic escalation, no missed tickets.
